6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fear of downtime is the number one reason businesses put off moving to cloud hosting. And that’s fair — even an hour of outage feels like an emergency when your site generates leads, processes orders, or handles customer questions.
Here’s what most people don’t realize: if you plan the migration correctly, there’s no downtime. None. Your visitors never see an error page. Emails keep flowing. Forms keep working. The whole switch happens in the background, and you’re the only one who knows it happened.
But migrations do go wrong — usually because someone rushed it, skipped testing, or forgot about email. Here’s the full process done right, along with the mistakes that actually matter for your business.
What to Have Ready Before You Start
Timing matters. Don’t start a migration on a Friday afternoon or the week before a big sale. Pick a low-traffic window — a Tuesday or Wednesday works well for most businesses.
What to gather first:

• Your hosting control panel login (cPanel, Plesk, or whatever you use)

• Your domain registrar login — often a different company than your host

• FTP or SSH access to your current server

• Database credentials if you’re running WordPress, WooCommerce, or any CMS
A full backup. Files, databases, email accounts, DNS records — all of it. Don’t assume your host has a backup. Download a copy and keep it yourself. This is your safety net if anything goes sideways.
Pro tip: Screenshot your current DNS records before touching anything. Wrong DNS records are the single most common cause of migration problems — and you can’t fix what you can’t remember.
Map out what’s connected to your domain. Website files and database, obviously. But also: email (hosting provider, Google Workspace, or something else?), any subdomains, SSL certificates, cron jobs, and third-party services tied to your server’s IP.
Step 1: Prep the New Cloud Server
Your cloud provider sets up a clean environment matching — or exceeding — your current one. Same PHP version, same database engine, same web server software (or newer, if your apps can handle it).
At Qaaf Cloud, this usually takes a few hours, and nothing changes for your live site while it happens — the new server runs completely on its own.
This is also when you lock down security: automatic updates, firewall rules, fail2ban for SSH. Get all of it done before any data moves over.
Step 2: Move Everything to the New Server
Time to transfer your files and database. A few ways to do it:

• WordPress/WooCommerce: Tools like Duplicator or All-in-One WP Migration package your whole site into one file you upload and extract on the new server.

• Bigger sites or custom apps: rsync over SSH is faster and more reliable for transferring files directly.

• Database: Export with mysqldump or phpMyAdmin, then import on the new server. Even large datasets usually take just minutes.
Important: Don’t touch your DNS yet. Your old server is still running the live site. The new server is just a private copy — only you can see it.
Step 3: Test Everything on the New Server
This is the step people skip, and it’s the one that prevents disasters.
Edit your computer’s hosts file to point your domain to the new server’s IP. Now only you can see the new version — everyone else still sees the old one.
Check:

• Does the homepage load with all graphics and styles intact?

• Do all internal links work?

• Does the contact form submit correctly?

• If you sell online, does checkout work end to end?

• Is SSL working with no browser warnings?

• Do cron jobs and scheduled tasks run?

• Can you send and receive email?
Common issues at this stage: hardcoded file paths, mixed-content warnings (HTTP resources on an HTTPS page), and database connection errors from mismatched credentials.
Step 4: Lower Your DNS TTL
A small step with a big payoff. A day or two before switching, lower your DNS TTL from the default (usually 86,400 seconds / 24 hours) down to around 300 seconds (5 minutes).
TTL tells ISPs how long to cache your DNS records. A shorter TTL means your change propagates across the internet much faster — most visitors reach the new server within minutes instead of waiting up to a full day.
Step 5: Switch DNS and Go Live
This is the actual cutover. Log into your domain registrar and point the A record (or nameservers) to your new cloud server’s IP.
With a lowered TTL, most visitors land on the new server within 5–30 minutes. During that window some people may briefly hit the old server and some the new one — but since both hold identical content, nobody notices.
Keep your old server running for at least 48–72 hours after the switch. Some DNS resolvers are slow to update, and you want a fallback if anything breaks.
Step 6: Post-Migration Checks
Once DNS has fully propagated (give it 24 hours to be safe), run your test checklist again — this time on the live site:

• All pages load correctly

• Forms deliver to the right email addresses

• Payment processing works (run a test transaction if you can)

• Email accounts send and receive normally

• Google Analytics and Search Console are tracking correctly

• Third-party integrations (CRMs, marketing tools, APIs) connect without issues
Only cancel your old hosting plan once you’re fully confident everything checks out. Not before.
Common Mistakes Businesses Make
Forgetting about email. This is the big one. If your email was hosted with your old provider, switching DNS without setting up email on the new server first kills your email entirely. Set up email on the new server before the switch — or move to a dedicated service like QaafMail or Google Workspace.
Skipping the backup. Worth repeating: don’t assume your host has a backup. Some do, some don’t, some are weeks stale. Make your own and keep it somewhere safe.
Changing too much at once. Don’t redesign your site, update your CMS, or add plugins on migration day. Migrate first. Let it settle for a week. Then make changes — that way, if something breaks, you know exactly what caused it.
Ignoring SSL certificates. Your existing SSL may be tied to your old server. Make sure a new certificate is live and working on the cloud server before DNS switches, or visitors will see security warnings — and some browsers will block the site outright.
How Long Does It Actually Take?
For a typical WordPress or WooCommerce site, the full process takes 24–48 hours — most of that is DNS propagation. Setting up the server, copying files, and testing usually takes 3–6 hours depending on site size.
Larger sites with custom apps, multiple databases, or complex configurations can take longer. For some enterprise clients, we’ve prepped and tested for a full week before the final cutover.
At Qaaf Cloud, we handle the entire migration for you — server setup, file transfer, testing, DNS, and post-migration checks. You never have to touch a terminal.
Frequently Asked Questions
Will I lose any data during the move? No, as long as it’s done properly. The new server copies your files and database — it doesn’t remove them from the old one. Originals stay put until you’re confident everything works, and you can delete them whenever you choose.
Can I migrate an e-commerce site without losing orders? Yes. We run one final database sync right before the DNS switch to capture any orders placed during the migration window. Nothing gets lost.
Do I need to update my domain registrar? You’ll need to update the A record or nameservers in your DNS settings at your registrar. Your hosting or migration team can walk you through exactly what to change.
What if something breaks after the move? If you kept your old server running, you can point DNS back to it within minutes. That’s exactly why we recommend keeping the old host active for at least 72 hours post-migration.
